title: sealed classes should avoid having protected members
description: "sealed" classes should not have "protected" membersAnalyze your codeintentionality-clearmaintainabilityCode SmellconfusingWhy is this an issue?The difference betweenprivateandprotectedvisibility is that child classes can see and useprotectedmembers, but they cannot seeprivateones. Since asealedclass cannot have children, marking its membersprotectedis confusingly pointless.Noncompliant code examplepublic sealed class MySealedClass { protected string name = "Fred"; // Noncompliant protected void SetName(string name) // Noncompliant { // ... } }Compliant solutionpublic sealed class MySealedClass { private string name = "Fred"; public void SetName(string name) { // ..
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

conditions: Class contains protected members (fields, methods, properties)

exclusion_conditions: Members are private, public, or internal
logic.reasoning: Detects sealed classes that have protected members, which is pointless since sealed classes cannot be inherited. Protected members in sealed classes should be either private (for encapsulation) or public/internal (for external access). The protected access modifier serves no purpose in sealed classes.

should_trigger: public sealed class MySealedClass { protected string name = 'Fred'; }
sealed class ConfigManager { protected readonly string configPath; }
sealed class Test { protected virtual void LoadConfig() { } }
should_not_trigger: public sealed class MySealedClass { private string name = 'Fred'; }
public class NonSealedClass { protected string name = 'Valid'; }
sealed class ValidConfigManager { internal void LoadConfig() { } }
